one_line_takeaway: Fine-tuned and external prompt-injection defenses (StruQ, SecAlign, and five commercial/open prompt guards) achieve high attack rejection by latching onto surface heuristics -- suffix position, attack-associated tokens, and training-topic membership -- causing severe false-refusal and accuracy collapse (up to 90% suffix refusal, up to 50-point trigger-induced refusal gaps, and up to 18.8-point accuracy drops) on entirely benign inputs.
key_finding: High attack-rejection performance masks large benign-input damage. Position bias is the dominant shortcut: in Table 1/2, suffix-task refusal rate after defense fine-tuning reaches up to 90% (e.g., Llama-3+StruQ on MMLU 2nd position RR=90.0% vs 6.0% at 1st position) while accuracy on the second question collapses (e.g., MMLU 2nd position accuracy 5.0% for Llama-3+StruQ vs 60.0% at 1st position); a 5-question ablation (Figure 3, Mistral+StruQ) shows response rate falling from 0.99 (position 1) to 0.55 (position 5) and accuracy from ~0.50 to ~0.23-0.27 for later positions, monotonic with position. Token trigger bias: inserting a single InjecGuard trigger token raises RR by up to 40-50 percentage points for some models/guards (Table 3), e.g. LakeraGuard RR 9.1%->58.5% (+49.4), FMOPS 65.3%->86.7% (+21.4), ProtectAIv2 13.8%->53.9% (+40.1), while a matched non-trigger control does not raise RR (e.g. Mistral+SecAlign trigger gap is slightly negative, -2.65). A trigger-removal ablation (Table 5) causally confirms this: removing trigger tokens from the same InjecGuard prompts cuts RR sharply, e.g. ProtectAIv2 53.9%->23.8% (Delta -30.1), LakeraGuard 58.5%->47.7% (Delta -10.9). Topic generalization: Table 4 shows average accuracy across GPQA/MMLU/AIME dropping after defense tuning even with zero adversarial content, e.g. Llama-3 Base 31.6 -> +StruQ 12.8 (-18.8), +SecAlign 22.4 (-3.1); Mistral Base 23.3 -> +StruQ 12.1 (-11.2), +SecAlign 10.1 (-13.2). A secondary mechanism (Figure 2) shows defended models produce systematically shorter chain-of-thought outputs than the base model on GPQA/MMLU ('reasoning collapse' via output-length truncation), which the authors argue explains part of the utility drop.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is an empirical diagnostic study showing that fine-tuning-based prompt-injection defenses (StruQ, SecAlign) and external prompt-guard classifiers (ProtectAIv2, LakeraGuard, PromptGuard2, Deepset, FMOPS) learn surface-level shortcut heuristics rather than detecting malicious intent. The authors formalize three hypotheses -- H1 Position Bias, H2 Token Trigger Bias, H3 Topic Generalization Bias -- construct controlled diagnostic datasets that vary exactly one surface factor while keeping semantic intent strictly benign, and run a systematic evaluation across two base models (Llama-3, Mistral, instruction-tuned, no additional task fine-tuning) crossed with the defense pipelines. They also release code/data (github.com/AiChiMoCha/MEval).
threat_model: Not an attack paper: the paper does not propose or execute new prompt-injection attacks. It instead adopts prompt injection as background (direct: attacker-controlled user input; indirect: attacker-controlled retrieved content, citing Perez & Ribeiro 2022 and Greshake et al. 2023 as prior work) and probes the FAILURE MODE of existing defenses on entirely benign inputs. The 'adversary' in their diagnostic setup is really the defense's own miscalibration: benign prompts are perturbed along a single surface axis (suffix position in a concatenated prompt, insertion of an attack-associated trigger token such as 'sudo'/'override', or membership in a topical domain absent from defense training data) with intent held constant and benign.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations (Section 7): the study is a focused empirical analysis under specific, well-defined evaluation conditions rather than an exhaustive survey of all possible defenses; it uses a concrete snapshot of present-day models (Llama-3, Mistral), benchmarks, and two fine-tuning defenses (StruQ, SecAlign) plus five prompt guards, so results may not generalize to other defense designs or deployment contexts; H3's topic-generalization claim relies on the natural domain mismatch between GPQA/MMLU/AIME and defense training corpora rather than an explicit train/held-out topic split constructed by the authors, which the paper itself flags ('Although no explicit topic split is applied...'). No new attacks are proposed and the paper does not evaluate whether the identified biases could be exploited adversarially (e.g., evading rejection by avoiding trigger tokens), which the authors leave to future work (counterfactual benign-but-attack-like training examples, interactive/multi-turn evaluation, and finer-grained behavioral signals like calibration).

datasets_used: InjecGuard trigger-stress set (113 benign prompts with attack-associated tokens)
quantitative_results: Position bias: Llama-3+StruQ MMLU 2nd-position refusal rate 90.0% vs 6.0% at 1st position; 2nd-position accuracy 5.0% vs 60.0% at 1st position (Table 2)
Position bias 5-question ablation (Mistral+StruQ): response rate drops from 0.99 (position 1) to 0.55 (position 5); accuracy drops from ~0.50 to ~0.23-0.27 across positions (Figure 3)

Token trigger bias: inserting a single trigger token raises refusal rate by 40-50 percentage points, e.g. LakeraGuard 9.1%->58.5% (+49.4), FMOPS 65.3%->86.7% (+21.4), ProtectAIv2 13.8%->53.9% (+40.1) (Table 3)
Trigger-removal ablation: removing trigger tokens cuts refusal rate, e.g. ProtectAIv2 53.9%->23.8% (Delta -30.1), LakeraGuard 58.5%->47.7% (Delta -10.9) (Table 5)

Topic generalization / alignment tax: average accuracy across GPQA/MMLU/AIME, Llama-3 Base 31.6 -> +StruQ 12.8 (-18.8), +SecAlign 22.4 (-3.1); Mistral Base 23.3 -> +StruQ 12.1 (-11.2), +SecAlign 10.1 (-13.2) (Table 4)
Position bias, single-position baseline: Llama-3+StruQ GPQA accuracy drops from 30.0 (Base) to 16.0 (-14.0) with refusal rate rising from 4.0% to 40.0% (Table 1)
